FULL CONTENT:
# Multimodal Healthcare AI: Identifying and Designing Clinically Relevant Vision-Language Applications for Radiology

###### Abstract.

Recent advances in AI combine large language models (LLMs) with vision encoders that bring forward unprecedented technical capabilities to leverage for a wide range of healthcare applications. Focusing on the domain of radiology, vision-language models (VLMs) achieve good performance results for tasks such as generating radiology findings based on a patient’s medical image, or answering visual questions (e.g., “Where are the nodules in this chest X-ray?”). However, the clinical utility of potential applications of these capabilities is currently underexplored. We engaged in an iterative, multidisciplinary design process to envision clinically relevant VLM interactions, and co-designed four VLM use concepts: Draft Report Generation, Augmented Report Review, Visual Search and Querying, and Patient Imaging History Highlights. We studied these concepts with 13 radiologists and clinicians who assessed the VLM concepts as valuable, yet articulated many design considerations. Reflecting on our findings, we discuss implications for integrating VLM capabilities in radiology, and for healthcare AI more generally.

## 1. Introduction

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly recognized as an important application in radiology (Huang et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib58); Liu et al., [2023b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib83); Patel et al., [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib102); Strohm et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib122)). In particular, the latest advancements in the creation and adaptation of multimodal foundation models (e.g., BioViL(-T) (Bannur et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib9); Boecking et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib18)), ELIXR (Xu et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib138)), MAIRA (Hyland et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib59)), Med-PaLM M (Tu et al., [2023a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib129))) invite high expectations of how the use of AI may transform clinical practice through efficiency and quality gains (Strohm et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib122)); and improved overall patient care. By leveraging rich, multimodal data that particularly characterizes the healthcare domain, advanced AI models can achieve impressive new and improved capabilities. In this work, we focus particularly on the combination of large language models (LLMs) with vision capabilities – as so called vision-language models (VLMs). In the context of radiology imaging, this modality combination enables tasks such as: automatically generating a radiology report from a medical image (e.g.,  (Huang et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib58); Hyland et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib59); Yu et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib149))); using text queries to answer questions about a radiology image (cf.  (Xu et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib138))); or detecting errors in a radiology report text through its comparison with the image.

Despite great AI advances both in natural language processing and image-based analysis, translating recent research and development successes into clinical practice remains challenging (Coiera, [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib33); Galsgaard et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib45); Ontika et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib98); Osman Andersen et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib101); Verma et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib133); Yu et al., [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib150); Tulk Jesso et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib131); Strohm et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib122); Yildirim et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib146); Zaj\kac et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib151)). Factors hindering successful AI implementation in radiology are wide ranging and include: skepticism due to inconsistent AI performance; lack of trust and overreliance in AI-generated outputs; and the need for clinical effectiveness trials (cf.  (Galsgaard et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib45))).
A key underlying factor is uncertainty about the value that AI applications bring to clinical practice. In what has been described as “a race for getting the technology right before exposing human-end users to new promising AI tools” (Osman Andersen et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib101)), the field of AI has been criticized for its development “in a vacuum” (Miller et al., [2017](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib89)), disconnected from well-defined needs of intended users or use contexts (Liao et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib80); Thieme et al., [2023b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib127)). Seeking to close the gap between technical proof-of-concepts and lab experiments towards the successful integration and deployment of AI-enabled systems within routine care requires the adoption of human-centered, participatory approaches (Ontika et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib99); Thieme et al., [2023a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib126)). This involves engagement with relevant stakeholders throughout AI system development, starting as early as the ideation and problem formulation stages (Ismail et al., [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib60); Kross and Guo, [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib70); Morrison et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib92); Yildirim et al., [2023b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib145); Wilcox et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib135); Cai et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib26)).

Within this broader context, we set out to better understand the design space of VLMs in healthcare, specifically in the context of radiology. Radiology imaging workflows involve referring clinicians who request an imaging test for a patient; and radiologists who examine the image and describe their findings and clinical impression. The resulting report goes back to referring clinicians to inform patient care and treatment (Langlots, [2015](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib72)). Building on the recent advances in AI research, we focused on designing the right thing (Buxton, [2010](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib23)): What might be clinically relevant use cases for VLMs to enhance radiology imaging workflows for radiologists and clinicians? Would radiologists want to engage with a draft report generated by AI? Would clinicians find it useful to have report findings visually annotated on an image? What questions might radiologists and clinicians ask if they could query a patient X-ray or CT scan?

As a team of human-computer interaction (HCI) researchers, AI researchers, radiologists and clinicians, we engaged in an iterative design process to explore these questions. We conducted a three-phase study. The first phase involved in-depth discussions and brainstorming sessions within our team to elicit our clinical team members’ domain expertise, and ideate use cases with VLM capabilities. We discussed how radiologists interpret images and write reports, and how clinicians review these to make patient care decisions. We brainstormed VLM-based interactions using sketches, scenarios and wireflows to identify what would be useful and acceptable. In the second phase, we selected four specific use cases to further detail as design concepts: Draft Report Generation, Augmented Report Review, Visual Search and Querying, and Patient Imaging History Highlights. In the third phase, we recruited 13 radiologists and clinicians to conduct user feedback sessions probing whether and how these concepts might be useful for clinical practice, and potential concerns.

Overall, participants perceived the VLM concepts as valuable, but articulated many design requirements for these to be usable and acceptable. Particularly, they shared expectations of AI performance, workflow integration (e.g., well-defined, tool-based interactions rather than open-ended queries), and a desire for context-specificity.

This paper makes two main contributions.
First, we identify and design VLM use cases to support radiology workflows, and offer initial insights into the perceived value of these concepts. Second, we present a reflective account of our design process as a case study of early phase AI innovation with clinical stakeholders, from brainstorming to prioritization, concept generation and initial assessment.
We discuss the design implications and future research directions for integrating VLM capabilities into radiology, and healthcare more generally.

## 2. Related Work

### 2.1. VLMs: Multimodal Foundation Models

Recently, considerable excitement has developed around a new class of AI models that have been termed foundation models (FMs) (Bommasani et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib19)). These models are trained on broad data at immense scale, which results in powerful general-purpose models that can be adapted and more flexibly (re)used for a wide range of tasks and domains – including healthcare  (Brown et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib21); Singhal et al., [2023a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib120); Thieme et al., [2023b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib127); Wójcik, [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib136)).
While FMs can in principle be developed for any data modality (text, image, audio, video, etc.) or their combination, we have seen most advances with large language models (LLMs) that demonstrate impressive capabilities to generate coherent human-like text. Prominent examples include OpenAI’s GPT-4 (OpenAI, [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib100)) and Google’s LaMDA (Collins and Ghahramani, [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib34)) models that power conversation-based AI innovations such as ChatGPT (AI, [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib2)), Microsoft’s Copilot (Microsoft, [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib88)), and Google Bard (Google, [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib49)).

In its basic function, an LLM generates statistically likely continuations of word sequences (Shanahan, [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib116)). For example, given a specific text fragment such as “Pneumonia is…” , the LLM may complete this fragment with “an infection that inflames the air sacs in one or both lungs”, because it is statistically a likely continuation given the words’ distribution in the vast collective corpus of human-generated (English) texts that the model was trained on. As a result, latest generations of LLMs (e.g., PaLM 2 (Anil et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib5)),
FLAN-T5 (Chung et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib31)), LLaMA 2 (Touvron et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib128))), and especially those trained on additional medical data (e.g., Med-PALM 2 (Singhal et al., [2023b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib121))), do not only interpret and respond in plain language. Having clinical representations encoded (Singhal et al., [2023a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib120)), they also exhibit a certain ‘comprehension’ in the medical domain (Corrado and Matias, [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib37)) as illustrated in their ability to correctly answer medical exam questions (Oh et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib97); Nori et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib95); Singhal et al., [2023a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib120), [b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib121)). As a result, LLMs are being explored for healthcare tasks such as: medical knowledge extraction (Preston et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib106)), literature search and medical article writing (Gu et al., [2023c](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib52); Lecler et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib75); Yang et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib140)); medical text simplification (Jeblick et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib62)) and clinical notes summarization (Krishna et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib69); Nabla, [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib93); Nuance-Microsoft, [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib96)); and as medical question-answering (Singhal et al., [2023b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib121)) and chatbot applications (Lee et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib76)).

Most recently, LLMs are combined with other modalities (Corrado and Matias, [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib37)), such as medical images (e.g., BioViL(-T) (Bannur et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib9); Boecking et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib18)), MAIRA (Hyland et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib59))) to better leverage rich, multimodal data that particularly characterizes healthcare; seeking to achieve new, improved or more efficient AI architectures and capabilities (Tu et al., [2023a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib129); Xu et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib138)). Most relevant to our work are AI models that leverage both radiology images and their associated report text (e.g.,  (Hyland et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib59); Xu et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib138))). Combining an LLM with an image encoder, a vision-language model (VLM) permits tasks such as: automatic generation of report text from a radiology image; text-image retrieval (e.g., Show me examples of left lower lobe pneumonia); visual question-answering (e.g., Does the patient have lung nodules or an infection?); or error detection in reports (e.g., detecting clinical findings in the image that are not reported in the text).

While prior work suggests the applicability of new AI capabilities as “AI Mentor” (Xu et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib138)) or “Autopilot for Radiologists” (Langlotz, [2019](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib73)), how these could be usefully configured to enrich human-AI radiology workflows warrants further study. Furthermore, many critical challenges need to be addressed to ensure safe and responsible VLM system design for clinical practice: The growing complexity of the underlying AI models makes it difficult, if not impossible, to understand their workings, or recognize when the AI might fail (Bommasani et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib19); Moor et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib90); Thieme et al., [2023b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib127)). Other issues include questions around domain specificity and quality of model input data (Gilbert et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib48); Lecler et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib75)) as well as societal biases that are inherent in that data (Bernhardt et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib13)), which increase with model scale and multimodality (Bommasani et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib19)), risking harms by exacerbating health disparities and social inequalities (Bender et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib12); Singhal et al., [2023a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib120); Thieme et al., [2023b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib127); Verma et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib133)). And while LLMs tend to show robust performance to out-of-distribution cases, they are sensitive to the phrasing of prompts; generate hallucinations (Jeblick et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib62); Lee et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib76))); or give high confidence indications even for wrong results (Sasha Luccioni and Rogers, [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib112)); posing significant challenges for AI trust and adoption (Gilbert et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib48)).

Our work seeks to bring a human-centered approach to the VLM-assisted radiology workflows by engaging radiologists and clinicians in early phase brainstorming and concept development.

### 2.2. Human-Centered Medical AI

Developing AI systems for healthcare is a complex space with many, wide-ranging sociotechnical challenges (Beede et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib10); Jacobs et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib61); Ghosh et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib47); Andersen et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib4); Zaj\kac et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib151)), spanning: (i) concerns about patient autonomy and ability to explicitly consent or withdraw from healthcare data uses, and its privacy protection in AI development or use (Thieme et al., [2020a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib124); Wilcox et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib135)); (ii) investigations into AI workflow integration (Burgess et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib22); Beede et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib10); Calisto et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib28)) and how best to configure clinician-AI relationships to effectively empower care providers (Gu et al., [2023b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib51); Thieme et al., [2023a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib126); Hirsch et al., [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib55); Yang et al., [2019b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib142); Yildirim et al., [2024b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib148)); as well as (iii) challenges around acceptance, trust and adoption of AI insights into clinical practice (Yang et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib140); Jacobs et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib61); Matthiesen et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib87); Sendak et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib115); Henry et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib53)). This is mostly addressed in the field of eXplainable AI (XAI) through research into AI transparency via explanations and other mechanisms to help clinicians contest (Hirsch et al., [2017](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib54)) or learn about AI outputs (Cai et al., [2019b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib25)) to be able to develop an appropriate mental model of AI capabilities and their limitations. Where uses of AI are especially proposed to support health screening, triage or treatment recommendations, research explores (iv) risks of inequality and unfair discrimination, which extends to clinical trial design (Chien et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib30)). All this further requires (v) robust evaluation frameworks and carefully defined AI model or system performance metrics (Liang et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib77)); and is interlinked with (vi) broader organizational challenges and regulatory approval requirements that pose additional questions about clinical accountability and taking medical-legal responsibility for any AI-assisted decision by individual users, healthcare institutions, or insurance providers (e.g.,  (Gilbert et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib48); Petersen et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib103); Procter et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib107); Zaj\kac et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib151))).

Within this vast, growing space, our research and design exploration within medical AI imaging (e.g., ophthalmology (Bach et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib8); Beede et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib10)), pathology (Cai et al., [2019a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib24); Gu et al., [2023b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib51), [a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib50); Lindvall et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib81))), specifically in radiology (Atad et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib6); Bernstein et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib14); Calisto et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib28), [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib29), [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib27); Ontika et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib98); Verma et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib133); Xie et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib137)), seeks to better understand – early within AI development processes – if and how specific, anticipated VLM capabilities could be beneficial in assisting clinical workflows.

### 2.3. Designing AI with Domain Stakeholders

HCI research highlights the difficulty of eliciting input from domain stakeholders in AI design and development, especially in early ideation and problem formulation phases to inform what is the right thing to design (Piorkowski et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib105); Kross and Guo, [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib70); Delgado et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib40); Buxton, [2010](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib23)). Prior work noted that stakeholders with little to no background in data science or AI (e.g., domain experts, UX designers, policymakers, etc) might be involved in the design of an AI system’s user interface, but rarely in conversations around the objective of the underlying model or the overall problem formulation (Delgado et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib40); Yildirim et al., [2023b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib145); Wang et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib134); Robertson and Salehi, [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib110); Deng et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib42)). Recently, a growing body of work in HCI and AI has called for human-centered approaches for broadening participation in AI design to meaningfully engage domain stakeholders to brainstorm and reflect on whether an envisioned future technology is in fact addressing the right problem in the first place (Delgado et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib41); Kuo et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib71); Corbett et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib36); Bell et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib11); Zhang et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib152); Cooper et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib35); Liao and Vaughan, [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib79)).

However, designing AI-based systems presents unique challenges even for experienced practitioners (Yang et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib141); Subramonyam et al., [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib123); Liao et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib78)).
Recent work investigating best practices for designing AI products highlights that effective innovation teams work with AI capabilities to scaffold cross-disciplinary ideation (Morrison et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib92); Yildirim et al., [2023b](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib145); Yang et al., [2019a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib139); Liu et al., [2023a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib82); Yildirim et al., [2023a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib144), [2022](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib143)). Inspired by matchmaking (Bly and Churchill, [1999](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib17)), this approach proposes considering user needs and AI capabilities simultaneously to explore matches in a problem-solution space.
Several researchers note that these capability abstractions, sketches, and prototypes serve as boundary objects to help bridge the knowledge gap between AI experts and domain stakeholders, allowing domain stakeholders to gain an understanding of what AI can do to articulate their desired futures (Yang et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib140); Cai et al., [2021](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib26); Ayobi et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib7); Yildirim et al., [2024a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib147)).
Researchers also point out that innovation teams often focus on use cases that require high task expertise (e.g., clinical decision making), where near-perfect AI performance is needed for a concept to be useful (Yildirim et al., [2023a](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib144); Yang et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib141); Feng et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib43)). Instead, researchers suggest focusing on where moderately performing, imperfect AI can still create value. We draw on this literature to explore VLMs as a design material (Yang et al., [2020](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib141); Morris et al., [2023](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib91)) for radiology workflows, investigating clinically relevant and valuable use cases for radiologists and clinicians.

## 3. Overview of Radiology Workflows

![Refer to caption](x1.png)

Radiology workflows unfold across many clinician roles (Figure [1](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#S3.F1 "Figure 1 ‣ 3. Overview of Radiology Workflows ‣ Multimodal Healthcare AI: Identifying and Designing Clinically Relevant Vision-Language Applications for Radiology")). First, referring clinicians request an imaging study for a patient (e.g., a chest X-ray). Next, radiographers perform patient scans, and radiology coordinators may prioritize and assign patient images to radiologists. Next, radiologists examine patient images, and document their findings – descriptions of normal or abnormal observations, such as lesions or nodules – and their clinical impression – a summary that synthesizes the findings and suggest possible causes or further tests. Referring clinicians then review the radiology report, and may consult radiologists for further questions or clarifications before making care decisions. In some cases, patient images are brought to multidisciplinary team meetings (MDT) to discuss patient treatment (Langlots, [2015](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib72)).

A radiology report (Figure [6](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#S10.F6 "Figure 6 ‣ 10. Appendix A: EXAMPLE RADIOLOGY REPORT AND PROTOTYPE FLOWS ‣ Multimodal Healthcare AI: Identifying and Designing Clinically Relevant Vision-Language Applications for Radiology") in the Appendix) typically consists of a Background section that describes the patient information and the clinical question that referring clinicians seek to answer, and Findings and Impression sections that communicates radiologists’ interpretation (Kahn Jr et al., [2009](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib67)).
Different imaging modalities have different workflows. For instance, plain (2D) imaging, such as X-rays, are high volume and fast-paced, taking minutes to review (Cowan et al., [2013](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib38)). Complex (3D) imaging on the other hand, such as CTs and MRIs, take more time (10-20 minutes) and cognitive effort (Cowan et al., [2013](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib38)).
Reports are often in the form of prose (sometimes called narrative report), while there is also research that calls for structured reporting approaches (e.g., short, bullet-point style sentences) for improved clarity (Ganeshan et al., [2018](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib46)).
Reports are usually written using voice dictation, often utilizing templates or draft reports produced by radiology trainees (interns or residents in the US context) in hospital settings.

Depending on the imaging modality and context, clinicians may review images –especially plain images such as X-rays– before a radiology report becomes available. For example, intensive care physicians immediately review X-rays that are taken to assess if a feeding tube is inserted correctly (Law, [2014](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib74)). Regardless of whether acted upon or not, all images require a radiology report as it serves as a legal document in a patient’s record (Clinger et al., [1988](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib32)). A major challenge within the radiology workflow is the sheer volume of scans, leading to a backlog of unreported images (Rimmer, [2017](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib109)). Wait times might be a few days to a week for radiology reports (Naik et al., [2001](https://arxiv.org/html/2402.14252v1#bib.bib94)). enriched_points.expanded_content: Multimodal AI systems integrate imaging data with genomics, pathology, and clinical notes, enhancing diagnostic accuracy and decision-making in radiology. This approach reflects a more holistic understanding of patient care.
AI-driven personalized medicine utilizes patient-specific data to tailor treatment plans, improving outcomes by predicting responses to therapies based on genetic and phenotypic profiles.
AI enhances preventive care by analyzing vast datasets to identify risk factors, enabling early intervention strategies and promoting proactive health management among at-risk populations.
